section-47-1-b.content: have reasonable cause to suspect that a child who lives, or is found, in their area is suffering, or is likely to suffer, significant harm,

additional_content: is the subject of an emergency protection order; or
is in police protection; ...
the authority shall make, or cause to be made, such enquiries as they consider necessary to enable them to decide whether they should take any action to safeguard or promote the child’s welfare. ....

section-47-2.content: Where a local authority have obtained an emergency protection order with respect to a child, they shall make, or cause to be made, such enquiries as they consider necessary to enable them to decide what action they should take to safeguard or promote the child’s welfare.

section-47-3.content: The enquiries shall, in particular, be directed towards establishing—

section-47-3-b.content: whether, in the case of a child—

section-47-3-c.content: whether, in the case of a child who has been taken into police protection, it would be in the child’s best interests for the authority to ask for an application to be made under section 46(7).

additional_content: make any application to court under this Act;
exercise any of their other powers under this Act;
exercise any of their powers under section 11 of the Crime and Disorder Act 1998 (child safety orders); or
(where the authority is a local authority in Wales) exercise any of their powers under the Social Services and Well-being (Wales) Act 2014;
with respect to the child;
with respect to whom an emergency protection order has been made; and
who is not in accommodation provided by or on behalf of the authority,
it would be in the child’s best interests (while an emergency protection order remains in force) for him to be in such accommodation; and

section-47-4.content: Where enquiries are being made under subsection (1) with respect to a child, the local authority concerned shall (with a view to enabling them to determine what action, if any, to take with respect to him) take such steps as are reasonably practicable—

section-47-4-a.content: to obtain access to him; or

section-47-4-b.content: to ensure that access to him is obtained, on their behalf, by a person authorised by them for the purpose,

section-47-4.additional_content: unless they are satisfied that they already have sufficient information with respect to him.

section-47-5.content: Where, as a result of any such enquiries, it appears to the authority that there are matters connected with the child’s education which should be investigated, they shall consultthe local authority (as defined in section 579(1) of the Education 1996), if different, specified in subsection (5ZA).

section-47-5ZA.content: The local authority referred to in subsection (5) is—
additional_content: maintain any school at which the child is a pupil, or
make arrangements for the provision of education for the child otherwise than at school pursuant to section 19or 19Aof the Education Act 1996, or

section-47-5A.content: For the purposes of making a determination under this section as to the action to be taken with respect to a child, a local authority shall, so far as is reasonably practicable and consistent with the child’s welfare—

section-47-6.content: Where, in the course of enquiries made under this section—

section-47-6-a.content: any officer of the local authority concerned; or

section-47-6-b.content: any person authorised by the authority to act on their behalf in connection with those enquiries—

additional_content: is refused access to the child concerned; or
is denied information as to his whereabouts,

the authority shall apply for an emergency protection order, a child assessment order, a care order or a supervision order with respect to the child unless they are satisfied that his welfare can be satisfactorily safeguarded without their doing so.

section-47-7.content: If, on the conclusion of any enquiries or review made under this section, the authority decide not to apply for an emergency protection order, a child assessment order, a care order or a supervision order they shall—

section-47-7-a.content: consider whether it would be appropriate to review the case at a later date; and

section-47-7-b.content: if they decide that it would be, determine the date on which that review is to begin.

section-47-8.content: Where, as a result of complying with this section, a local authority conclude that they should take action to safeguard or promote the child’s welfare they shall take that action (so far as it is both within their power and reasonably practicable for them to do so).

section-47-9.content: Where a local authority are conducting enquiries under this section, it shall be the duty of any person mentioned in subsection (11) to assist them with those enquiries (in particular by providing relevant information and advice) if called upon by the authority to do so.

section-47-10.content: Subsection (9) does not oblige any person to assist a local authority where doing so would be unreasonable in all the circumstances of the case.

section-47-11-d.content: anyintegrated care board,Local Health Board, Special Health Authority..., National Health Service trust orNHSfoundation trust; and

section-47-11-e.content: any person authorised by the Secretary of State for the purposes of this section.

section-47-12.content: Where a local authority are making enquiries under this section with respect to a child who appears to them to be ordinarily resident within the area of another authority, they shall consult that other authority, who may undertake the necessary enquiries in their place.

section-48.title: Powers to assist in discovery of children who may be in need of emergency protection.

section-48-1.content: Where it appears to a court making an emergency protection order that adequate information as to the child’s whereabouts—

section-48-1-a.content: is not available to the applicant for the order; but

section-48-1.additional_content: it may include in the order a provision requiring that other person to disclose, if asked to do so by the applicant, any information that he may have as to the child’s whereabouts.

section-48-2.content: No person shall be excused from complying with such a requirement on the ground that complying might incriminate him or his spouseor civil partnerof an offence; but a statement or admission made in complying shall not be admissible in evidence against either of them in proceedings for any offence other than perjury.

section-48-3.content: An emergency protection order may authorise the applicant to enter premises specified by the order and search for the child with respect to whom the order is made.

section-48-4.content: Where the court is satisfied that there is reasonable cause to believe that there may be another child on those premises with respect to whom an emergency protection order ought to be made, it may make an order authorising the applicant to search for that other child on those premises.

section-48-5-a.content: an order has been made under subsection (4);

section-48-5-b.content: the child concerned has been found on the premises; and

section-48-5-c.content: the applicant is satisfied that the grounds for making an emergency protection order exist with respect to him,

section-48-5.additional_content: the order shall have effect as if it were an emergency protection order.

section-48-6.content: Where an order has been made under subsection (4), the applicant shall notify the court of its effect.

section-48-7.content: A person shall be guilty of an offence if he intentionally obstructs any person exercising the power of entry and search under subsection (3) or (4).

section-48-8.content: A person guilty of an offence under subsection (7) shall be liable on summary conviction to a fine not exceeding level 3 on the standard scale.

section-48-9.content: Where, on an application made by any person for a warrant under this section, it appears to the court—

section-48-9-a.content: that a person attempting to exercise powers under an emergency protection order has been prevented from doing so by being refused entry to the premises concerned or access to the child concerned; or

section-48-9-b.content: that any such person is likely to be so prevented from exercising any such powers,

section-48-9.additional_content: it may issue a warrant authorising any constable to assist the person mentioned in paragraph (a) or (b) in the exercise of those powers using reasonable force if necessary.

section-48-10.content: Every warrant issued under this section shall be addressed to, and executed by, a constable who shall be accompanied by the person applying for the warrant if—

section-48-10-b.content: the court by whom the warrant is issued does not direct otherwise.

section-48-11.content: A court granting an application for a warrant under this section may direct that the constable concerned may, in executing the warrant, be accompanied by a registered medical practitioner, registered nurse orregistered midwifeif he so chooses.

section-48-11A.content: The reference in subsection (11) to a registered midwife is to such a midwife who is also registered in the Specialist Community Public Health Nurses' Part of the register maintained under article 5 of the Nursing and Midwifery Order 2001.

section-48-12.content: An application for a warrant under this section shall be made in the manner and form prescribed by rules of court.

section-48-13.content: Wherever it is reasonably practicable to do so, an order under subsection (4), an application for a warrant under this section and any such warrant shall name the child; and where it does not name him it shall describe him as clearly as possible.

section-49.title: Abduction of children in care etc.

section-49-1.content: A person shall be guilty of an offence if, knowingly and without lawful authority or reasonable excuse, he—

section-49-1-a.content: takes a child to whom this section applies away from the responsible person;

section-49-1-b.content: keeps such a child away from the responsible person; or

section-49-1-c.content: induces, assists or incites such a child to run away or stay away from the responsible person.

section-49-2.content: This section applies in relation to a child who is—

section-49-2-b.content: the subject of an emergency protection order; or

section-49-2.additional_content: and in this section “the responsible person” means any person who for the time being has care of him by virtue of the care order, the emergency protection order, or section 46, as the case may be.

section-49-3.content: A person guilty of an offence under this section shall be liable on summary conviction to imprisonment for a term not exceeding six months, or to a fine not exceeding level 5 on the standard scale, or to both.

section-50-1.content: Where it appears to the court that there is reason to believe that a child to whom this section applies—

section-50-1-a.content: has been unlawfully taken away or is being unlawfully kept away from the responsible person;

section-50-1-b.content: has run away or is staying away from the responsible person; or

section-50-1.additional_content: the court may make an order under this section (“a recovery order”).

section-50-2.content: This section applies to the same children to whom section 49 applies and in this section “the responsible person” has the same meaning as in section 49.

section-50-3-a.content: operates as a direction to any person who is in a position to do so to produce the child on request to any authorised person;

section-50-3-b.content: authorises the removal of the child by any authorised person;

section-50-3-c.content: requires any person who has information as to the child’s whereabouts to disclose that information, if asked to do so, to a constable or an officer of the court;

section-50-3-d.content: authorises a constable to enter any premises specified in the order and search for the child using reasonable force if necessary.

section-50-4.content: The court may make a recovery order only on the application of—

section-50-4-a.content: any person who has parental responsibility for the child by virtue of a care order or emergency protection order; or

section-50-4-b.content: where the child is in police protection, the designated officer.

section-50-5.content: A recovery order shall name the child and—

section-50-5-a.content: any person who has parental responsibility for the child by virtue of a care order or emergency protection order; or

section-50-5-b.content: where the child is in police protection, the designated officer.

section-50-6.content: Premises may only be specified under subsection (3)(d) if it appears to the court that there are reasonable grounds for believing the child to be on them.

items: “ an authorised person ” means—

section-50-7-a.content: any person specified by the court;

additional_content: after the recovery order is made; and
by a person who has parental responsibility for the child by virtue of a care order or an emergency protection order,
to exercise any power under a recovery order; and

section-50-8.content: Where a person is authorised as mentioned in subsection (7)(c)—

section-50-8-a.content: the authorisation shall identify the recovery order; and

section-50-8-b.content: any person claiming to be so authorised shall, if asked to do so, produce some duly authenticated document showing that he is so authorised.

section-50-9.content: A person shall be guilty of an offence if he intentionally obstructs an authorised person exercising the power under subsection (3)(b) to remove a child.

section-50-10.content: A person guilty of an offence under this section shall be liable on summary conviction to a fine not exceeding level 3 on the standard scale.

section-50-11.content: No person shall be excused from complying with any request made under subsection (3)(c) on the ground that complying with it might incriminate him or his spouseor civil partnerof an offence; but a statement or admission made in complying shall not be admissible in evidence against either of them in proceedings for an offence other than perjury.

section-50-12.content: Where a child is made the subject of a recovery order whilst being looked after by a local authority, any reasonable expenses incurred by an authorised person in giving effect to the order shall be recoverable from the authority.

section-50-13.content: A recovery order shall have effect in Scotland as if it had been made by the Court of Session and as if that court had had jurisdiction to make it.

section-50-14.content: In this section “the court”, in relation to Northern Ireland, means a magistrates’ court within the meaning of the Magistrates’ Courts (Northern Ireland) Order 1981.

section-51-1.content: Where it is proposed to use a voluntary home orprivatechildren’s home to provide a refuge for children who appear to be at risk of harm, the Secretary of State may issue a certificate under this section with respect to that home.

section-51-2.content: Where a local authority or voluntary organisation arrange for a foster parent to provide such a refuge, the Secretary of State may issue a certificate under this section with respect to that foster parent.

section-51-3.content: In subsection (2) “foster parent” means a person who is, or who from time to time is, a local authority foster parent or a foster parent with whom children are placed by a voluntary organisation.

section-51-4.content: The Secretary of State may by regulations—

section-51-4-a.content: make provision as to the manner in which certificates may be issued;

section-51-4-b.content: impose requirements which must be complied with while any certificate is in force; and

section-51-4-c.content: provide for the withdrawal of certificates in prescribed circumstances.

section-51-5.content: Where a certificate is in force with respect to a home, none of the provisions mentioned in subsection (7) shall apply in relation to any person providing a refuge for any child in that home.

section-51-6.content: Where a certificate is in force with respect to a foster parent, none of those provisions shall apply in relation to the provision by him of a refuge for any child in accordance with arrangements made by the local authority or voluntary organisation.

section-51-7-b.content: articles 9, 10 and 11 of the Children’s Hearing (Scotland) Act 2011 (Consequential and Transitional Provisions and Savings) Order 2013, so far as they apply to anything done in England and Wales;

section-51-7-c.content: section 32(3) of the Children and Young Persons Act 1969 (compelling, persuading, inciting or assisting any person to be absent from detention, etc.), so far as it applies in relation to anything done in England and Wales;

section-51-7-d.content: section 2 of the Child Abduction Act 1984.

section-52-1.content: Without prejudice to section 93 or any other power to make such rules, rules of court may be made with respect to the procedure to be followed in connection with proceedings under this Part.

section-52-2.content: The rules may, in particular make provision—

section-52-2-a.content: as to the form in which any application is to be made or direction is to be given;

section-52-2-b.content: prescribing the persons who are to be notified of—

section-52-2-c.content: as to the content of any such notification and the manner in which, and person by whom, it is to be given.

additional_content: the making, or extension, of an emergency protection order; or
the making of an application under section 45(4) or (8) or 46(7); and

section-52-3.content: The Secretary of State may by regulations provide that, where—

section-52-3-a.content: an emergency protection order has been made with respect to a child;

section-52-3-b.content: the applicant for the order was not the local authority within whose area the child is ordinarily resident; and

section-52-3-c.content: that local authority are of the opinion that it would be in the child’s best interests for the applicant’s responsibilities under the order to be transferred to them,

section-52-3.additional_content: that authority shall (subject to their having complied with any requirements imposed by the regulations) be treated, for the purposes of this Act, as though they and not the original applicant had applied for, and been granted, the order.

section-52-4.content: Regulations made under subsection (3) may, in particular, make provision as to—

section-52-4-a.content: the considerations to which the local authority shall have regard in forming an opinion as mentioned in subsection (3)(c); and

section-52-4-b.content: the time at which responsibility under any emergency protection order is to be treated as having been transferred to a local authority.

section-53.title: Provision of community homes by local authorities.

section-53-1.content: Every local authority shall make such arrangements as they consider appropriate for securing that homes (“community homes”) are available—

section-53-1-a.content: for the care and accommodation of children looked after by them; and

section-53-1-b.content: for purposes connected with the welfare of children (whether or not looked after by them),

section-53-1.additional_content: and may do so jointly with one or more other local authorities.

section-53-2.content: In making such arrangements, a local authority shall have regard to the need for ensuring the availability of accommodation—

section-53-2-b.content: which is suitable for different purposes and the requirements of different descriptions of children.

section-53-3.content: A community home may be a home—

section-53-3-a.content: provided,equipped, maintained and (subject to subsection (3A)) managedby a local authority; or

section-53-3-b.content: provided by a voluntary organisation but in respect of which a local authority and the organisation—

additional_content: propose that, in accordance with an instrument of management, theequipment, maintenance and (subject to subsection (3B)) managementof the home shall be the responsibility of the local authority; or
so propose that the management, equipment and maintenance of the home shall be the responsibility of the voluntary organisation.

section-53-3A.content: A local authority may make arrangements for the management by another person of accommodation provided by the local authority for the purpose of restricting the liberty of children.

section-53-3B.content: Where a local authority are to be responsible for the management of a community home provided by a voluntary organisation, the local authority may, with the consent of the body of managers constituted by the instrument of management for the home, make arrangements for the management by another person of accommodation provided for the purpose of restricting the liberty of children.

section-53-4.content: Where a local authority are to be responsible for the management of a community home provided by a voluntary organisation, the authority shall designate the home as a controlled community home.

section-53-5.content: Where a voluntary organisation are to be responsible for the management of a community home provided by the organisation, the local authority shall designate the home as an assisted community home.

section-53-6.content: Schedule 4 shall have effect for the purpose of supplementing the provisions of this Part.

section-55.title: Determination of disputes relating to controlled and assisted community homes.

section-55-1.content: Where any dispute relating to a controlled community home arises between the local authority specified in the home’s instrument of management and—

section-55-1-a.content: the voluntary organisation by which the home is provided; or

section-55-1-b.content: any other local authority who have placed, or desire or are required to place, in the home a child who is looked after by them,

section-55-1.additional_content: the dispute may be referred by either party to the Secretary of State for his determination.

section-55-2.content: Where any dispute relating to an assisted community home arises between the voluntary organisation by which the home is provided and any local authority who have placed, or desire to place, in the home a child who is looked after by them, the dispute may be referred by either party to the Secretary of State for his determination.

section-55-3.content: Where a dispute is referred to the Secretary of State under this section he may, in order to give effect to his determination of the dispute, give such directions as he thinks fit to the local authority or voluntary organisation concerned.

section-55-4.content: This section applies even though the matter in dispute may be one which, under or by virtue of Part II of Schedule 4, is reserved for the decision, or is the responsibility, of—

section-55-4-a.content: the local authority specified in the home’s instrument of management; or

section-55-4-b.content: (as the case may be) the voluntary organisation by which the home is provided.

section-55-5.content: Where any trust deed relating to a controlled or assisted community home contains provision whereby a bishop or any other ecclesiastical or denominational authority has power to decide questions relating to religious instruction given in the home, no dispute which is capable of being dealt with in accordance with that provision shall be referred to the Secretary of State under this section.

section-55-6.content: In this Part “trust deed”, in relation to a voluntary home, means any instrument (other than an instrument of management) regulating—

section-55-6-a.content: the maintenance, management or conduct of the home; or

section-55-6-b.content: the constitution of a body of managers or trustees of the home.

section-56.title: Discontinuance by voluntary organisation of controlled or assisted community home.

section-56-1.content: The voluntary organisation by which a controlled or assisted community home is provided shall not cease to provide the home except after giving to the Secretary of State and the local authority specified in the home’s instrument of management not less than two years’ notice in writing of their intention to do so.

section-56-2.content: A notice under subsection (1) shall specify the date from which the voluntary organisation intend to cease to provide the home as a community home.

section-56-3.content: Where such a notice is given and is not withdrawn before the date specified in it, the home’s instrument of management shall cease to have effect on that date and the home shall then cease to be a controlled or assisted community home.

section-56-4.content: Where a notice is given under subsection (1) and the home’s managers give notice in writing to the Secretary of State that they are unable or unwilling to continue as its managers until the date specified in the subsection (1) notice, the Secretary of State may by order—

section-56-4-a.content: revoke the home’s instrument of management; and

section-56-4-b.content: require the local authority who were specified in that instrument to conduct the home until—

additional_content: the date specified in the subsection (1) notice; or
such earlier date (if any) as may be specified for the purposes of this paragraph in the order,
as if it were a community home provided by the local authority.

section-56-5.content: Where the Secretary of State imposes a requirement under subsection (4)(b)—

section-56-5-a.content: nothing in the trust deed for the home shall affect the conduct of the home by the local authority;

section-56-5-b.content: the Secretary of State may by order direct that for the purposes of any provision specified in the direction and made by or under any enactment relating to community homes (other than this section) the home shall, until the date or earlier date specified as mentioned in subsection (4)(b), be treated as a controlled or assisted community home;

section-56-5-c.content: except in so far as the Secretary of State so directs, the home shall until that date be treated for the purposes of any such enactment as a community home provided by the local authority; and

section-56-5-d.content: on the date or earlier date specified as mentioned in subsection (4)(b) the home shall cease to be a community home.

section-57.title: Closure by local authority of controlled or assisted community home.

section-57-1.content: The local authority specified in the instrument of management for a controlled or assisted community home may give—

section-57-1-b.content: the voluntary organisation by which the home is provided,

section-57-1.additional_content: not less than two years’ notice in writing of their intention to withdraw their designation of the home as a controlled or assisted community home.

section-57-2.content: A notice under subsection (1) shall specify the date (“the specified date”) on which the designation is to be withdrawn.

section-57-3-a.content: a notice is given under subsection (1) in respect of a controlled or assisted community home;

section-57-3-b.content: the home’s managers give notice in writing to the Secretary of State that they are unable or unwilling to continue as managers until the specified date; and

section-57-3-c.content: the managers’ notice is not withdrawn,

section-57-3.additional_content: the Secretary of State may by order revoke the home’s instrument of management from such date earlier than the specified date as may be specified in the order.

section-57-4.content: Before making an order under subsection (3), the Secretary of State shall consult the local authority and the voluntary organisation.

section-57-5.content: Where a notice has been given under subsection (1) and is not withdrawn, the home’s instrument of management shall cease to have effect on—

section-57-5-b.content: where an earlier date has been specified under subsection (3), that earlier date,

section-57-5.additional_content: and the home shall then cease to be a community home.

section-58.title: Financial provisions applicable on cessation of controlled or assisted community home or disposal etc. of premises.

section-58-1-a.content: the instrument of management for a controlled or assisted community home is revoked or otherwise ceases to have effect under section . . . , 56(3) or (4)(a) or 57(3) or (5); or

section-58-1-b.content: any premises used for the purposes of such a home are (at any time after 13th January 1987) disposed of, or put to use otherwise than for those purposes,

section-58-1.additional_content: the proprietor shall become liable to pay compensation (“the appropriate compensation”) in accordance with this section.

section-58-2.content: Where the instrument of management in force at the relevant time relates—

section-58-2-a.content: to a controlled community home; or

section-58-2-b.content: to an assisted community home which, at any time before the instrument came into force, was a controlled community home,

section-58-2.additional_content: the appropriate compensation is a sum equal to that part of the value of any premises which is attributable to expenditure incurred in relation to the premises, while the home was a controlled community home, by the authority who were then the responsible authority.

section-58-3.content: Where the instrument of management in force at the relevant time relates—

section-58-3-a.content: to an assisted community home; or

section-58-3-b.content: to a controlled community home which, at any time before the instrument came into force, was an assisted community home,

section-58-3.additional_content: the appropriate compensation is a sum equal to that part of the value of the premises which is attributable to the expenditure of money provided by way of grant under section 82, section 65 of the Children and Young Persons Act 1969 or section 82 of the Child Care Act 1980.

section-58-4.content: Where the home is, at the relevant time, conducted in premises which formerly were used as an approved school or were an approved probation hostel or home, the appropriate compensation is a sum equal to that part of the value of the premises which is attributable to the expenditure—

section-58-4-a.content: of sums paid towards the expenses of the managers of an approved school under section 104 of the Children and Young Persons Act 1933; . . .

section-58-4-b.content: of sums paid under section 51(3)(c) of the Powers of Criminal Courts Act 1973or section 20(1)(c) of the Probation Service Act 1993in relation to expenditure on approved probation hostels or homes.or

section-58-4-c.content: of sums paid under section 3, 5 or 9 of the Criminal Justice and Court Services Act 2000 in relation to expenditure on approved premises (within the meaning of Part I of that Act).

section-58-5.content: The appropriate compensation shall be paid—

section-58-5-a.content: in the case of compensation payable under subsection (2), to the authority who were the responsible authority at the relevant time; and

section-58-5-b.content: in any other case, to the Secretary of State.

items: “ disposal ” includes the grant of a tenancy and any other conveyance, assignment, transfer, grant, variation or extinguishment of an interest in or right over land, whether made by instrument or otherwise;
“ premises ” means any premises or part of premises (including land) used for the purposes of the home and belonging to the proprietor;
“ the proprietor ” means—

section-58-6-a.content: the voluntary organisation by which the home is, at the relevant time, provided; or

section-58-6-b.content: if the premises are not, at the relevant time, vested in that organisation, the persons in whom they are vested;

section-58-7.content: For the purposes of this section an event of a kind mentioned in subsection (1)(b) shall be taken to have occurred—

section-58-7-a.content: in the case of a disposal, on the date on which the disposal was completed or, in the case of a disposal which is effected by a series of transactions, the date on which the last of those transactions was completed;

section-58-7-b.content: in the case of premises which are put to different use, on the date on which they first begin to be put to their new use.

section-58-8.content: The amount of any sum payable under this section shall be determined in accordance with such arrangements—

section-58-8-a.content: as may be agreed between the voluntary organisation by which the home is, at the relevant time, provided and the responsible authority or (as the case may be) the Secretary of State; or

section-58-8-b.content: in default of agreement, as may be determined by the Secretary of State.

section-58-9.content: With the agreement of the responsible authority or (as the case may be) the Secretary of State, the liability to pay any sum under this section may be discharged, in whole or in part, by the transfer of any premises.

section-58-10.content: This section has effect regardless of—

section-58-10-a.content: anything in any trust deed for a controlled or assisted community home;

section-58-10-b.content: the provisions of any enactment or instrument governing the disposition of the property of a voluntary organisation.

section-59.title: ` Provision of accommodation by voluntary organisations.

section-59-1.content: Where a voluntary organisation provide accommodation for a child, they shall do so by—

section-59-1-aa.content: maintaining him ina children's home in respect of which a person is registered under Part 2 of the Care Standards Act 2000or Part 1 of the Regulation and Inspection of Social Care (Wales) Act 2016;

section-59-1-f.content: making such other arrangements (subject to subsection (3)) as seem appropriate to them.

additional_content: a family;
a relative of his; or
any other suitable person,
on such terms as to payment by the organisation and otherwise as the organisation may determine(subject to section 49 of the Children Act 2004);

section-59-1A.content: Where under subsection (1)(aa) avoluntary organisationmaintains a child in a home provided, equipped and maintained byan appropriate national authorityunder section 82(5), it shall do so on such terms asthat national authoritymay from time to time determine.

section-59-2.content: Theappropriate national authoritymay make regulations as to the placing of children with foster parents by voluntary organisations ... .

section-59-3.content: Theappropriate national authoritymay make regulations as to the arrangements which may be made under subsection (1)(f) ... .

section-59-3A.content: Regulationsmade in relation to Englandunder subsection (2) or (3) may in particular make provision which (with any necessary modifications) is similar to that which may be made under section 22C by virtue of any of paragraphs 12B, 12E and 12F of Schedule 2.

section-59-3B.content: Regulations made in relation to Wales under subsection (2) or (3) may in particular make provision which (with any necessary modifications) is similar to that which may be made under sections 81 or 87 of the Social Services and Well-being (Wales) Act 2014, including provision which may be made under section 87 in accordance with the examples given in sections 89, 92 and 93 of that Act.

section-59-4.content: Theappropriate national authoritymay make regulations requiring any voluntary organisation who are providing accommodation for a child—

section-59-4-b.content: to consider any representations (including any complaint) made to them by any person falling within a prescribed class of person,

section-59-4.additional_content: in accordance with the provisions of the regulations.

section-59-5A.content: Regulationsmade in relation to Englandunder subsection (4) may, in particular—

section-59-5B.content: Regulations made in relation to Wales under subsection (4) may in particular make provision which (with any necessary modifications) is similar to that which may be made under sections 99, 100 or 102 of the Social Services and Well-being (Wales) Act 2014.

section-59-6.content: Regulations under subsections (2) to (4) may provide that any person who, without reasonable excuse, contravenes or fails to comply with a regulation shall be guilty of an offence and liable on summary conviction to a fine not exceeding level 4 on the standard scale.

section-59-7.content: In this Part “appropriate national authority” means—

section-59-7-a.content: in relation to England, the Secretary of State; and

section-59-7-b.content: in relation to Wales, the Welsh Ministers.

section-60-3.content: In this Act “voluntary home” means a children’s home which is carried on by a voluntary organisation but does not include a community home.

section-60-4.content: Schedule 5 shall have effect for the purpose of supplementing the provisions of this Part.

section-61-1.content: Where a child is accommodated by or on behalf of a voluntary organisation, it shall be the duty of the organisation—

section-61-1-a.content: to safeguard and promote his welfare;

section-61-1-b.content: to make such use of the services and facilities available for children cared for by their own parents as appears to the organisation reasonable in his case; and

section-61-1-c.content: to advise, assist and befriend him with a view to promoting his welfare when he ceases to be so accommodated.

section-61-2.content: Before making any decision with respect to any such child the organisation shall, so far as is reasonably practicable, ascertain the wishes and feelings of—

section-61-2-c.content: any person who is not a parent of his but who has parental responsibility for him; and

section-61-2-d.content: any other person whose wishes and feelings the organisation consider to be relevant,

section-61-2.additional_content: regarding the matter to be decided.

section-61-3.content: In making any such decision the organisation shall give due consideration—

section-61-3-a.content: having regard to the child’s age and understanding, to such wishes and feelings of his as they have been able to ascertain;

section-61-3-b.content: to such other wishes and feelings mentioned in subsection (2) as they have been able to ascertain; and

section-61-3-c.content: to the child’s religious persuasion, racial origin and cultural and linguistic background.

section-62-1.content: Every local authority shall satisfy themselves that any voluntary organisation providing accommodation—

section-62-1-a.content: within the authority’s area for any child; or

section-62-1-b.content: outside that area for any child on behalf of the authority,

section-62-1.additional_content: are satisfactorily safeguarding and promoting the welfare of the children so provided with accommodation.

section-62-2.content: Every local authority shall arrange for children who are accommodated within their area by or on behalf of voluntary organisations to be visited, from time to time, in the interests of their welfare.

section-62-3-a.content: requiring every child who is accommodated within a local authority’s area, by or on behalf of a voluntary organisation, to be visited by an officer of the authority—

section-62-3-b.content: imposing requirements which must be met by any local authority, or officer of a local authority, carrying out functions under this section.

additional_content: in prescribed circumstances; and
on specified occasions or within specified periods; and

section-62-4.content: Subsection (2) does not apply in relation to community homes.

section-62-5.content: Where a local authority are not satisfied that the welfare of any child who is accommodated by or on behalf of a voluntary organisation is being satisfactorily safeguarded or promoted they shall—

section-62-5-a.content: unless they consider that it would not be in the best interests of the child, take such steps as are reasonably practicable to secure that the care and accommodation of the child is undertaken by—

section-62-5-b.content: consider the extent to which (if at all) they should exercise any of their functions with respect to the child.

additional_content: a parent of his;
any person who is not a parent of his but who has parental responsibility for him; or
a relative of his; and

section-62-6.content: Any person authorised by a local authority may, for the purpose of enabling the authority to discharge their duties under this section—

section-62-6-a.content: enter, at any reasonable time, and inspect any premises in which children are being accommodated as mentioned in subsection (1) or (2);

section-62-6-c.content: require any person to furnish him with such records of a kind required to be kept by regulations made undersection 22 of the Care Standards Act 2000or section 20 of the Health and Social Care Act 2008, or section 27 of the Regulation and Inspection of Social Care (Wales) Act 2016 (anaw 2)(in whatever form they are held), or allow him to inspect such records, as he may at any time direct.

section-62-7.content: Any person exercising the power conferred by subsection (6) shall, if asked to do so, produce some duly authenticated document showing his authority to do so.

section-62-8.content: Any person authorised to exercise the power to inspect records conferred by subsection (6)—

section-62-8-a.content: shall be entitled at any reasonable time to have access to, and inspect and check the operation of, any computer and any associated apparatus or material which is or has been in use in connection with the records in question; and

additional_content: the person by whom or on whose behalf the computer is or has been so used; or
any person having charge of, or otherwise concerned with the operation of, the computer, apparatus or material,
to afford him such assistance as he may reasonably require.

section-62-9.content: Any person who intentionally obstructs another in the exercise of any power conferred by subsection (6) or (8) shall be guilty of an offence and liable on summary conviction to a fine not exceeding level 3 on the standard scale.

section-62-10.content: This section does not apply in relation to any voluntary organisation which is an institution within the further education sector, as defined in section 91 of theFurther and Higher Education Act 1992,a 16 to 19 Academyor a school.

section-63-11.content: Schedule 6 shall have effect with respect toprivatechildren’s homes.

section-63-12.content: Schedule 7 shall have effect for the purpose of setting out the circumstances in which a person may foster more than three childrenwithout being treated—

section-63-12-a.content: for the purposes of this Act and the Care Standards Act 2000,as carrying on a children’s home; and

section-63-12-b.content: for the purposes of the Regulation and Inspection of Social Care (Wales) Act 2016, as providing a care home service within the meaning of Part 1 of that Act.

section-64.title: Welfare of children in children’s homes.

section-64-1.content: Where a child is accommodated in aprivatechildren’s home, it shall be the duty of the person carrying on the home to—

section-64-1-a.content: safeguard and promote the child’s welfare;

section-64-1-b.content: make such use of the services and facilities available for children cared for by their own parents as appears to that person reasonable in the case of the child; and

section-64-1-c.content: advise, assist and befriend him with a view to promoting his welfare when he ceases to be so accommodated.

section-64-2.content: Before making any decision with respect to any such child the person carrying on the home shall, so far as is reasonably practicable, ascertain the wishes and feelings of—

section-64-2-c.content: any other person who is not a parent of his but who has parental responsibility for him; and

section-64-2-d.content: any person whose wishes and feelings the person carrying on the home considers to be relevant,

section-64-2.additional_content: regarding the matter to be decided.

section-64-3.content: In making any such decision the person concerned shall give due consideration—

section-64-3-a.content: having regard to the child’s age and understanding, to such wishes and feelings of his as he has been able to ascertain;

section-64-3-b.content: to such other wishes and feelings mentioned in subsection (2) as he has been able to ascertain; and

section-64-3-c.content: to the child’s religious persuasion, racial origin and cultural and linguistic background.

section-64-4.content: Section 62, except subsection (4), shall apply in relation to any person who is carrying on aprivatechildren’s home as it applies in relation to any voluntary organisation.

section-65.title: 2 Persons disqualified from carrying on, or being employed in children’s homes.

section-65-1.content: A person who is disqualified (under section 68) from fostering a child privately shall not carry on, or be otherwise concerned in the management of, or have any financial interest in a children’s homein Walesunless he has—

section-65-1-a.content: disclosed tothe appropriate authoritythe fact that he is so disqualified; and

section-65-2.content: No person shall employ a person who is so disqualified in a children’s homein Walesunless he has—

section-65-2-a.content: disclosed tothe appropriate authoritythe fact that that person is so disqualified; and

section-65-3.content: Wherethe appropriate authority refuses to give its consent under this section, itshall inform the applicant by a written notice which states—

section-65-3-b.content: the applicant’s right to appeal under section 65A against the refusal to theFirst-tier Tribunal

section-65-3-c.content: the time within which he may do so.

section-65-4.content: Any person who contravenes subsection(A1), (A2),(1) or (2) shall be guilty of an offence and liable on summary conviction to imprisonment for a term not exceeding six months or to a fine not exceeding level 5 on the standard scale or to both.

section-65-5.content: Where a person contravenes subsection(A2) or(2) he shall not be guilty of an offence if he proves that he did not know, and had no reasonable grounds for believing, that the person whom he was employing was disqualified under section 68.

section-65-6.content: In this section and section 65A “appropriate authority” means—

section-65-6-a.content: in relation to England, theHer Majesty's Chief Inspector of Education, Children's Services and Skills; and

section-65-6-b.content: in relation to Wales, the National Assembly for Wales.

section-65A.title: Appeal against refusal of authority to give consent under section 65.
section-65A.content:
(1)An appeal against a decision of an appropriate authority under section 65 shall lie to theFirst-tier Tribunal.
(2)On an appeal the Tribunal may confirm the authority’s decision or direct it to give the consent in question.

section-66-1-a.content: “a privately fostered child” means a child who is under the age of sixteen and who is cared for, and provided with accommodationin their own homeby, someone other than—

section-66-1-b.content: “to foster a child privately” means to look after the child in circumstances in which he is a privately fostered child as defined by this section.

additional_content: a parent of his;
a person who is not a parent of his but who has parental responsibility for him; or
a relative of his; and

section-66-2.content: A child is not privately fostered child if the person caring for and accommodating him—

section-66-2-a.content: has done so for a period a period of less than 28 days; and

section-66-2-b.content: does not intend to do so for any longer period.

section-66-3-b.content: the exceptions made by paragraph 1 to 5 of Schedule 8.

section-66-4.content: In the case of a child who is disabled, subsection (1)(a) shall have effect as if for “sixteen”there were substituted “eighteen”.

section-66-4A.content: The Secretary of State may by regulations make provision as to the circumstances in which a person who provides accommodation to a child is, or is not, to be treated as providing him with accommodation in the person’s own home.

section-66-5.content: Schedule 8 shall have effect for the purposes of supplementing the provision made by this Part.

section-67-1.content: It shall be the duty of every local authority to satisfy themselves that the welfare of children who areor are proposed to beprivately fostered within their area is beingor will besatisfactorily safeguarded and promoted and to secure that such advice is given to thoseconcerned withthem as appears to the authority to be needed.

section-67-2.content: The Secretary of State may make regulations—

section-67-2-a.content: requiring every child who is privately fostered within a local authority’s area to be visited by an officer of the authority—

additional_content: in prescribed circumstances; and
on specified occasions or within specified periods; and

section-67-2A.content: Regulations under subsection (2)(b) may impose requirements as to the action to be taken by a local authority for the purposes of discharging their duty under subsection (1) where they have received notification of a proposal that a child be privately fostered.

section-67-3.content: Where any person who is authorised by a local authority to visitfor the purposehas reasonable cause to believe that—

section-67-3-a.content: any privately fostered child is being accommodated in premises within the authority’s area; or

section-67-3-b.content: it is proposed to accommodate any such child in any such premises,

section-67-3.additional_content: he may at any reasonable time inspect those premises and any children there.

section-67-4.content: Any person exercising the power under subsection (3) shall, if so required, produce some duly authenticated document showing his authority to do so.

section-67-5.content: Where a local authority are not satisfied that the welfare of any child who isor is proposed to beprivately fostered within their area is beingor will besatisfactorily safeguarded or promoted they shall—

section-67-5-a.content: unless they consider that it would not be in the best interests of the child, take such steps as are reasonably practicable to secure that the care and accommodation of the child is undertaken by—

section-67-5-b.content: consider the extent to which (if at all) they should exercise any of their functions under this Act with respect to the child.

additional_content: a parent of his;
any person who is not a parent of his but who has
parental responsibility for him; or
a relative of his; and

section-67-6.content: The Secretary of State may make regulations requiring a local authority to monitor the way in which the authority discharge their functions under this Part (and the regulations may in particular require the authority to appoint an officer for that purpose).

section-68.title: Persons disqualified from being private foster parents.

section-68-1.content: Unless he has disclosed the fact to the appropriate local authority and obtained their written consent, a person shall not foster a child privately if he is disqualified from doing so by regulations made by the Secretary of State for the purposes of this section.

section-68-2.content: The regulations may, in particular, provide for a person to be so disqualified where—

section-68-2-a.content: an order of a kind specified in the regulations has been made at any time with respect to him;

section-68-2-b.content: an order of a kind so specified has been made at any time with respect to any child who has been in his care;

section-68-2-c.content: a requirement of a kind so specified has been imposed at any time with respect to any such child, under or by virtue of any enactment;

section-68-2-d.content: he has been convicted of any offence of a kind so specified, or. . . discharged absolutely or conditionally for any such offence;

section-68-2-e.content: a prohibition has been imposed on him at any time under section 69 or under any other specified enactment;

section-68-2-f.content: his rights and powers with respect to a child have at any time been vested in a specified authority under a specified enactment.

section-68-2A.content: A conviction in respect of which a probation order was made before 1st October 1992 (which would not otherwise be treated as a conviction) is to be treated as a conviction for the purposes of subsection (2)(d).

section-68-3.content: Unless he has disclosed the fact to the appropriate local authority and obtained their written consent, a person shall not foster a child privately if—

section-68-3-a.content: he lives in the same household as a person who is himself prevented from fostering a child by subsection (1); or

section-68-3-b.content: he lives in a household at which any such person is employed.

section-68-3A.content: A person shall not foster a child privately if—

section-68-4.content: Where an authority refuse to give their consent under this section, they shall inform the applicant by a written notice which states—

section-68-4-b.content: the applicant’s right under paragraph 8 of Schedule 8 to appeal against the refusal; and

section-68-4-c.content: the time within which he may do so.

items: “ the appropriate authority ” means the local authority within whose area it is proposed to foster the child in question; and
“ enactment ” means any enactment having effect, at any time, in any part of the United Kingdom.

section-69-1.content: This section applies where a person—

section-69-1-a.content: proposes to foster a child privately; or

section-69-2.content: Where the local authority for the area within which the child is proposed to be, or is being, fostered are of the opinion that—

section-69-2-a.content: he is not a suitable person to foster a child;

section-69-2-b.content: the premises in which the child will be, or is being, accommodated are not suitable; or

section-69-2-c.content: it would be prejudicial to the welfare of the child for him to be, or continue to be accommodated by that person in those premises,

section-69-2.additional_content: the authority may impose a prohibition on him under subsection (3).

section-69-3.content: A prohibition imposed on any person under this subsection may prohibit him from fostering privately—

section-69-3-a.content: any child in any premises within the area of the local authority; or

section-69-3-b.content: any child in premises specified in the prohibition.

section-69-3-c.content: a child identified in the prohibition, in premises specified in the prohibition.

section-69-4.content: A local authority who have imposed a prohibition on any person under subsection (3) may, if they think fit, cancel the prohibition—

section-69-4-b.content: on an application made by that person,

section-69-4.additional_content: if they are satisfied that the prohibition is no longer justified.

section-69-5.content: Where a local authority impose a requirement on any person under paragraph 6 of Schedule 8, they may also impose a prohibition on him under subsection (3).

section-69-6.content: Any prohibition imposed by virtue of subsection (5) shall not have effect unless—

section-69-6-a.content: the time specified for compliance with the requirement has expired; and

section-69-6-b.content: the requirement has not been compiled with.

section-69-7.content: A prohibition imposed under this section shall be imposed by notice in writing addressed to the person on whom it is imposed and informing him of—

section-69-7-a.content: the reason for imposing the prohibition;

section-69-7-b.content: his right under paragraph 8 of Schedule 8 to appeal against the prohibition; and

section-69-7-c.content: the time within which he may do so.

section-70-1.content: A person shall be guilty of an offence if—

section-70-1-a.content: being required, under any provision made by or under this Part, to give any notice or information—

section-70-1-b.content: he refuses to allow a privately fostered child to be visited by a duly authorised officer of a local authority;

section-70-1-c.content: he intentionally obstructs another in the exercise of the power conferred by section 67(3);

section-70-1-e.content: he fails without reasonable excuse to comply with any requirement imposed by a local authority under this Part;

section-70-1-f.content: he accommodates a privately fostered child in any premises in contravention of a prohibition imposed by a local authority under this Part;

section-70-1-g.content: he knowingly causes to be published, or publishes, an advertisement which he knows contravenes paragraph 10 of Schedule 8.

additional_content: he fails without reasonable excuse to give the notice within the time specified in that provision; or
he fails without reasonable excuse to give the information within a reasonable time; or
he makes, or causes or procures another person to make, any statement in the notice or information which he knows to be false or misleading in a material particular;

section-70-2.content: Where a person contravenes section 68(3), he shall not be guilty of an offence under this section if he proves that he did not know, and had no reasonable ground for believing, that any person to whom section 68(1) applied was living or employed in the premises in question.

section-70-3.content: A person guilty of an offence under subsection (1)(a) shall be liable on summary conviction to a fine not exceeding level 5 on the standard scale.

section-70-4.content: A person guilty of an offence under subsection (1)(b), (c) or (g) shall be liable on summary conviction to a fine not exceeding level 3 on the standard scale.

section-70-5.content: A person guilty of an offence under subsection (1)(d) or (f) shall be liable on summary conviction to imprisonment for a term not exceeding six months, or to a fine not exceeding level 5 on the standard scale, or to both.

section-70-6.content: A person guilty of an offence under subsection (1)(e) shall be liable on summary conviction to a fine not exceeding level 4 on the standard scale.

section-70-7.content: If any person who is required, under any provision of this Part, to give a notice fails to give the notice within the time specified in that provision, proceedings for the offence may be brought at any time within six months from the date when evidence of the offence came to the knowledge of the local authority.

section-70-8.content: Subsection (7) is not affected by anything in section 127(1) of the Magistrates’ Courts Act 1980 (time limit for proceedings).

part-X.title: Child Minding and Day Care for Young Children

part-XA.title: Child Minding and Day Care for Children in. . . Wales

part-XI.title: Secretary of State’s Supervisory Functions and Responsibilities

section--80-.title: Inspection of children’s homesetc.by persons authorised bythe Appropriate National Authority.

section-80-1.content: TheAppropriate National Authoritymay cause to be inspected from time to time any—

section-80-1-b.content: premises in which a child who is being looked after by a local authority is living;

section-80-1-c.content: premises in which a child who is being accommodated by or on behalf of alocal authority in the exercise of education functions or avoluntary organisation is living;

section-80-1-d.content: premises in which a child who is being accommodated by or on behalf of aLocal Health Board, Special Health Authority..., National Health Service trust orNHSfoundation trustor pursuant to arrangements made by theAppropriate National Authority,NHS Englandoran integrated care boardunder the National Health Service Act 2006is living;

section-80-1-g.content: premises in which a privately fostered child, or child who is treated as a foster child by virtue of paragraph 9 of Schedule 8, is living or in which it is proposed that he will live;

section-80-1-h.content: premises on which any person is acting as a child minder;

section-80-1-j.content: care homein Englandor independent hospital used to accommodate children;

section-80-1-k.content: premises which are provided by a local authority and in which any service is provided by that authority under Part III;

section-80-1-l.content: school or collegeproviding accommodation for any child;

section-80-2.content: An inspection under this section shall be conducted by a person authorised to do so by theAppropriate National Authority.

section-80-3.content: An officer of a local authority shall not be so authorised except with the consent of that authority.

section-80-4.content: TheAppropriate National Authoritymay require any person of a kind mentioned in subsection (5) to furnishitwith such information, or allowitto inspect such records (in whatever form they are held), relating to—

section-80-4-a.content: any premises to which subsection (1) or, in relation to Scotland, subsection (1)(h) or (i) applies;

section-80-4-b.content: any child who is living in any such premises;

section-80-4-c.content: the discharge by theAppropriate National Authorityof any ofitsfunctions under this Act;or

section-80-4-d.content: the discharge by any local authority of any of their functions under this Act,

section-80-4.additional_content: as theAppropriate National Authoritymay at any time direct.

section-80-5-c.content: person carrying on aprivatechildren’s homein England;

section-80-5-d.content: proprietor of an independent schoolor governing body of any other school;

section-80-5-da.content: governing body of an institution designated under section 28 of theFurther and Higher Education Act 1992;

section-80-5-e.content: person fostering any privately fostered child or providing accommodation for a child on behalf of a local authority,...Local Health Board, Special Health Authority...National Health Service trust,NHSfoundation trustor voluntary organisation;

section-80-5-ea.content: person providing accommodation for a child pursuant to arrangements made by the Secretary of State,NHS Englandoran integrated care boardunder the National Health Service Act 2006;

section-80-5-g.content: person employed in a teaching or administrative capacity at any educational establishment (whether or not maintained bya local authority) at which a child is accommodated on behalf of a local authority. . . ;

section-80-5-i.content: person carrying on any home of a kind mentioned in subsection (1)(j).

section-80-5-j.content: person carrying on a fostering agency.

section-80-6.content: Any person inspecting any home or other premises under this section may—

section-80-6-b.content: make such examination into the state and management of the home or premises and the treatment of the children there as he thinks fit.

section-80-7.content: Any person authorised by theAppropriate National Authorityto exercise the power to inspect records conferred by subsection (4)—

section-80-7-a.content: shall be entitled at any reasonable time to have access to, and inspect and check the operation of, any computer and any associated apparatus or material which is or has been in use in connection with the records in question; and

additional_content: the person by whom or on whose behalf the computer is or has been so used; or
any person having charge of, or otherwise concerned with the operation of, the computer, apparatus or material,
to afford him such reasonable assistance as he may require.

section-80-8.content: A person authorised to inspect any premises under this section shall have a right to enter the premises for that purpose, and for any purpose specified in subsection (4), at any reasonable time.

section-80-9.content: Any person exercising that power shall, if so required, produce some duly authenticated document showing his authority to do so.

section-80-10.content: Any person who intentionally obstructs another in the exercise of that power shall be guilty of an offence and liable on summary conviction to a fine not exceeding level 3 on the standard scale.

section-80-11.content: TheAppropriate National Authoritymay by order provide for subsections (1), (4) and (6) not to apply in relation to such homes, or other premises, as may be specified in the order.

section-80-11A.content: But subsections (1), (4) and (6) do not apply if—

section-80-12.content: Without prejudice to section 104, any such order may make different provision with respect to each of those subsections.

items: “ college ” means an institution within the further education sector as defined in section 91 of the Further and Higher Education Act 1992 or a 16 to 19 Academy ;
“ fostering agency ” has the same meaning as in the Care Standards Act 2000;
“ further education corporation ” has the same meaning as in the Further and Higher Education Act 1992.
“ sixth form college corporation ” has the same meaning as in that Act.
“ proprietor ” has the same meaning as in the Education Act 1996.

section-80-14.content: In this section “Appropriate National Authority” means—

section-80-14-a.content: in relation to England, the Secretary of State; and

section-80-14-b.content: in relation to Wales, the Welsh Ministers.

section-82.title: Financial support by Secretary of State.

section-82-1.content: The Secretary of State may (with the consent of the Treasury) defray or contribute towards—

section-82-1-a.content: any fees or expenses incurred by any person undergoing approved child care training;

section-82-1-b.content: any fees charged, or expenses incurred, by any person providing approved child care training or preparing material for use in connection with such training; or

section-82-1-c.content: the cost of maintaining any person undergoing such training.

section-82-2.content: The Secretary of State may make grants to local authorities in respect of expenditure incurred by them in providing secure accommodation in community homes other than assisted community homes.

section-82-3-a.content: a grant has been made under subsection (2) with respect to any secure accommodation; but

section-82-3-b.content: the grant is not used for the purpose for which it was made or the accommodation is not used as, or ceases to be used as, secure accommodation,

section-82-3.additional_content: the Secretary of State may (with the consent of the Treasury) require the authority concerned to repay the grant, in whole or in part.

section-82-4.content: The Secretary of State may make grants to voluntary organisations towards—

section-82-4-a.content: expenditure incurred by them in connection with the establishment, maintenance or improvement of voluntary homes which, at the time when the expenditure was incurred—

section-82-4-b.content: expenses incurred in respect of the borrowing of money to defray any such expenditure.

additional_content: were assisted community homes; or
were designated as such; or

section-82-5.content: The Secretary of State may arrange for the provision, equipment and maintenance of homes for the accommodation of children who are in need of particular facilities and services which—

section-82-5-a.content: are or will be provided in those homes; and

section-82-5-b.content: in the opinion of the Secretary of State, are unlikely to be readily available in community homes.

items: “ child care training ” means training undergone by any person with a view to, or in the course of—

section-82-6-a.content: his employment for the purposes of any of the functions mentioned in section 83(9) or in connection with the adoption of children or with the accommodation of children in acare home or independent hospital; or

section-82-6-b.content: his employment by a voluntary organisation for similar purposes;

section-82-7.content: Any grant made under this section shall be of such amount, and shall be subject to such conditions, as the Secretary of State may (with the consent of the Treasury) determine.

section-83-1.content: The Secretary of State may conduct, or assist other persons in conducting, research into any matter connected with—

section-83-1-a.content: his functions, or the functions of local authorities, under the enactments mentioned in subsection (9);

section-83-1-c.content: the accommodation of children in acare home or independent hospital.

additional_content: the Child Safeguarding Practice Review Panel;
safeguarding partners (within the meaning given by section 16E(3) of the Children Act 2004) in relation to local authority areas in England;
child death review partners (within the meaning given by section 16Q(2) of the Children Act 2004) in relation to local authority areas in England;

section-83-2.content: Any local authority may conduct, or assist other persons in conducting, research into any matter connected with—

section-83-2-a.content: their functions under the enactments mentioned in subsection (9);

section-83-2-c.content: the accommodation of children in acare home or independent hospital.

section-83-3.content: Every local authority shall, at such times and in such form as the Secretary of State may direct, transmit to him such particulars as he may require with respect to—

section-83-3-a.content: the performance by the local authority of all or any of their functions—

section-83-3-b.content: the children in relation to whom the authority have exercised those functions.;...

additional_content: under the enactments mentioned in subsection (9); or
in connection with the accommodation of children in acare home or independent hospital; and

section-83-4.content: Every voluntary organisation shall, at such times and in such form as the Secretary of State may direct, transmit to him such particulars as he may require with respect to children accommodated by them or on their behalf.

section-83-4A.content: Particulars required to be transmitted under subsection (3) or (4) may include particulars relating to and identifying individual children.

section-83-5.content: The Secretary of State may directan officer of the family courtto transmit—

section-83-5-a.content: to such person as may be specified in the direction; and

section-83-5-b.content: at such times and in such form as he may direct,

section-83-5.additional_content: such particulars as he may require with respect to proceedings of the court which relate to children.

section-83-6.content: The Secretary of State shall in each year lay before Parliament a consolidated and classified abstract of the information transmitted to him under subsections (3) to (5).

section-83-7.content: The Secretary of State may institute research designed to provide information on which requests for information under this section may be based.

section-83-8.content: The Secretary of State shall keep under review the adequacy of the provision of child care training and for that purpose shall receive and consider any information from or representations made by—

section-83-8-a.content: the Central Council for Education and Training in Social Work;

section-83-8-b.content: such representatives of local authorities as appear to him to be appropriate; or

section-83-8-c.content: such other persons or organisations as appear to him to be appropriate,

section-83-8.additional_content: concerning the provision of such training.

section-83-9-b.content: the Children and Young Persons Acts 1933 to 1969;

section-83-9-c.content: section 116 of the Mental Health Act 1983 (so far as it relates to children looked after by local authorities);

section-83-9-ca.content: Part 1 of the Adoption and Children Act 2002;

section-83-9-cc.content: the Children and Young Persons Act 2008.

section-84.title: Local authority failure to comply with statutory duty: default power of Secretary of State.

section-84-1.content: If the Secretary of State is satisfied that any local authority has failed, without reasonable excuse, to comply with any of the duties imposed on them by or under this Act he may make an order declaring that authority to be in default with respect to that duty.

section-84-2.content: An order under subsection (1) shall give the Secretary of State’s reasons for making it.

section-84-3.content: An order under subsection (1) may contain such directions for the purpose of ensuring that the duty is complied with, within such period as may be specified in the order, as appear to the Secretary of State to be necessary.

section-84-4.content: Any such direction shall, on the application of the Secretary of State, be enforceable by mandamus.

section-85.title: Children accommodated by health authorities and local education authorities.

section-85-1.content: Where a child is provided with accommodationin Englandby any..., Special Health Authority,...National Health Service trustorNHSfoundation trust or by a local authorityin Englandin the exercise of education functions(“the accommodating authority”)—

section-85-1-a.content: for a consecutive period of at least three months; or

section-85-1-b.content: with the intention, on the part of that authority, of accommodating him for such a period,

section-85-1.additional_content: the accommodating authority shall notifythe appropriate officer ofthe responsible authority.

section-85-2.content: Where subsection (1) applies with respect to a child, the accommodating authority shall also notifythe appropriate officer ofthe responsible authority when they cease to accommodate the child.

section-85-2ZA.content: Where a child is provided with accommodation—
section-85-2ZA.additional_content: subsections (1) and (2) apply in relation to the Secretary of State,NHS Englandor (as the case may be)the integrated care boardas if it were the accommodating authority.

section-85-2A.content: In a case where the child isprovided with accommodation in England by a local authority in Englandin the exercise of education functions, subsections (1) and (2) apply only if the local authority providing the accommodation is different from the responsible authority.

section-85-3.content: In this section “the responsible authority” means—

section-85-3-a.content: the local authority appearing to the accommodating authority to be the authority within whose area the child was ordinarily resident immediately before being accommodated; or

section-85-3-b.content: where it appears to the accommodating authority that a child was not ordinarily resident within the area of any local authority, the local authority within whose area the accommodation is situated.

section-85-3A.content: In this section and sections 86 and 86A “the appropriate officer” means—

section-85-4.content: Wherethe appropriate officer of alocal authority in England has been notified under this section, or under section 120 of the Social Services and Well-being (Wales) Act 2014 (assessment of children accommodated by health authorities and education authorities),the local authorityshall—

section-85-4-a.content: take such steps as are reasonably practicable to enable them to determine whether the child’s welfare is adequately safeguarded and promoted while he is accommodated by the accommodating authority; and

section-85-4-b.content: consider the extent to which (if at all) they should exercise any of their functions under this Act with respect to the child.

section-85-5.content: For the purposes of subsection (4)(b), if the child is not in the area of the local authority, they must treat him as if he were in that area.

section-86.title: Children accommodated in care homes or independent hospitals.

section-86-1.content: Where a child is provided with accommodationin Englandin anycare home or independent hospital—

section-86-1-a.content: for a consecutive period of at least three months; or

section-86-1-b.content: with the intention, on the part of the person taking the decision to accommodate him, of accommodating him for such period,

section-86-1.additional_content: the person carrying onthe establishment in questionshall notifythe appropriate officer ofthe local authority within whose areathe establishmentis carried on.

section-86-2.content: Where subsection (1) applies with respect to a child, the person carrying onthe establishmentshall also notifythe appropriate officer ofthat authority when he ceases to accommodate the child inthe establishment.

section-86-3.content: Wherethe appropriate officer of a local authority hasbeen notified under this section,the local authorityshall—

section-86-3-a.content: take such steps as are reasonably practicable to enable them to determine whether the child’s welfare is adequately safeguarded and promoted while he is accommodated inthe establishment in question; and

section-86-3-b.content: consider the extent to which (if at all) they should exercise any of their functions under this Act with respect to the child.

section-86-4.content: If the person carrying on anycare home or independent hospitalfails, without reasonable excuse, to comply with this section he shall be guilty of an offence.

section-86-5.content: A person authorised by a local authorityin Englandmay enter anycare home or independent hospitalwithin the authority’s area for the purpose of establishing whether the requirements of this section have been complied with.

section-86-6.content: Any person who intentionally obstructs another in the exercise of the power of entry shall be guilty of an offence.

section-86-7.content: Any person exercising the power of entry shall, if so required, produce some duly authenticated document showing his authority to do so.

section-86-8.content: Any person committing an offence under this section shall be liable on summary conviction to a fine not exceeding level 3 on the standard scale.

section-86A.title: Visitors for children notified to local authority...
section-86A.content:
(1)This section applies if the appropriate officer of a local authorityin England—
(a)has been notified with respect to a child under section 85(1) or 86(1)“, or under section 120(2)(a) of the Social Services and Well-being (Wales) Act 2014; and
(b)has not been notified with respect to that child under section 85(2), 86(2), or under section 120(2)(b) of the Social Services and Well-being (Wales) Act 2014, as the case may be.
(2)The local authority must, in accordance with regulations made under this section, make arrangements for the child to be visited by a representative of the authority (“a representative”).
(3)It is the function of a representative to provide advice and assistance to the local authority on the performance of their duties under section 85(4) or, as the case may be, 86(3).
(4)Regulations under this section may make provision about—
(a)the frequency of visits under visiting arrangements;
(b)circumstances in which visiting arrangements must require a child to be visited; and
(c)additional functions of a representative.
(5)Regulations under this section are to be made by the Secretary of State....
(6)In choosing a representative a local authority must satisfy themselves that the person chosen has the necessary skills and experience to perform the functions of a representative.
(7)In this section “visiting arrangements” means arrangements made under subsection (2).

section-87.title: Welfare of children in boarding schools and colleges.

section-87-1.content: Where a school or college provides accommodation for any child, it shall be the duty of the relevant person to safeguard and promote the child’s welfare.

section-87-1A.content: For the purposes of this section and sections 87A to 87D, a school or college provides accommodation for a child if—

section-87-2.content: Subsection (1) does not apply in relation to a school or college which is a children’s home or care home.
